title: Archived Results at a Glance - Evaluation of Food-borne and Water-Borne Enteric Illness Activities at PHAC 2012-17 - Canada.ca
content:
## Archived Results at a Glance - Evaluation of Food-borne and Water-Borne Enteric Illness Activities at PHAC 2012-17
- PHAC’s activities include: Surveillance for food/water-borne illness and antimicrobial resistance; Outbreak detection and response; Risk modelling, knowledge synthesis, decision analysis; Laboratory services, genomics and bioinformatics, computing infrastructure to support laboratory activities; and Communication with the Canadian public.
- Surveillance for food/water-borne illness and antimicrobial resistance;
- Outbreak detection and response;
- Risk modelling, knowledge synthesis, decision analysis;
- Laboratory services, genomics and bioinformatics, computing infrastructure to support laboratory activities; and
- Communication with the Canadian public.
- These activities aim to provide information and tools to: Stakeholders so they can prevent, detect and respond to food/water-borne enteric illnesses; and, Canadians so they can protect themselves from food/water-borne enteric illnesses.
- Stakeholders so they can prevent, detect and respond to food/water-borne enteric illnesses; and,
- Canadians so they can protect themselves from food/water-borne enteric illnesses.
- Over the 2012-13 to 2016-17 period, the expenditures for these activities totalled approximately $86.6M.

## What the evaluation found
- Overall, PHAC contributes to Canada's strong food safety system through surveillance, genomics technology, and outbreak response.
- Stakeholders use PHAC’s resources to build their own skills, expand their resources, and get assistance to identify source of pathogens during outbreaks. However, there is room to improve how information and tools are presented and shared with stakeholders.
- PHAC is leading and supporting provinces with the transition to Whole Genome Sequencing. This method produces detailed data in a shorter timeframe and improves detection of outbreaks and characterization of pathogens. The transition is currently experiencing capacity challenges as it has increased the demand on PHAC’s resources and staff.
- PHAC’s response to outbreak notifications is consistently timely. PHAC’s approach for coordinating multijurisdictional outbreaks is viewed as a best practice.
- At the time of the evaluation, it was too early to measure the success of the new communication process implemented to improve the consistency in messaging from the Canadian Food Inspection Agency (CFIA) and PHAC on food-borne illness outbreaks.
- Canadians can benefit from increased awareness on how to prevent food/water- borne enteric illnesses.

## Recommendations and responses
- PHAC should update its plan for the Whole Genome Sequencing implementation taking into consideration the capacity constraints experienced in the initial phase of the transition within both PHAC and the provinces. Response: Building on the PulseNet Canada Genomics Roadmap, PHAC will assess critical capacity gaps impeding the adoption of genome sequencing.  PHAC will explore resourcing options and strategic engagement of partners to address the gaps identified.
PHAC should update its plan for the Whole Genome Sequencing implementation taking into consideration the capacity constraints experienced in the initial phase of the transition within both PHAC and the provinces.
Response: Building on the PulseNet Canada Genomics Roadmap, PHAC will assess critical capacity gaps impeding the adoption of genome sequencing.  PHAC will explore resourcing options and strategic engagement of partners to address the gaps identified.
- Improve access to upstream surveillance information and ensure the content of upstream information products is adapted to the needs of stakeholders. Response: PHAC will review opportunities (such as practices of other federal departments) to improve methods to reach target audiences to share information such as scientific reference material and relevant findings from surveillance and outbreak response programs. PHAC will implement new knowledge translation strategy with audience-specific information products targeted to stakeholders and Canadians. It will also develop a monitoring framework on how the strategy is performing.
Improve access to upstream surveillance information and ensure the content of upstream information products is adapted to the needs of stakeholders.
Response: PHAC will review opportunities (such as practices of other federal departments) to improve methods to reach target audiences to share information such as scientific reference material and relevant findings from surveillance and outbreak response programs. PHAC will implement new knowledge translation strategy with audience-specific information products targeted to stakeholders and Canadians. It will also develop a monitoring framework on how the strategy is performing.
- Monitor the effectiveness of CFIA/PHAC’s new coordinated communications process for outbreak investigations. Response: As part of the lessons learned, revise the standard operating procedure for the partner Outbreak Investigation Coordinating Committee post-outbreak debrief to include questions about the consistency and effectiveness of coordinated messaging.
Monitor the effectiveness of CFIA/PHAC’s new coordinated communications process for outbreak investigations.
Response: As part of the lessons learned, revise the standard operating procedure for the partner Outbreak Investigation Coordinating Committee post-outbreak debrief to include questions about the consistency and effectiveness of coordinated messaging.
- Explore how PHAC could support messaging to Canadians on food/water-borne illness prevention. Response: PHAC will clarify its role and contribution to the portfolio approach regarding the management of food safety messaging in order to prevent human illness from food-borne pathogens. PHAC will also draft an inventory/umbrella strategy of engagement activities across PHAC, working with Health Canada and CFIA to identify gaps and opportunities for increased outreach.
Explore how PHAC could support messaging to Canadians on food/water-borne illness prevention.
Response: PHAC will clarify its role and contribution to the portfolio approach regarding the management of food safety messaging in order to prevent human illness from food-borne pathogens. PHAC will also draft an inventory/umbrella strategy of engagement activities across PHAC, working with Health Canada and CFIA to identify gaps and opportunities for increased outreach.

## About this evaluation
This evaluation examined the performance of PHAC’s food/water-borne enteric illness activities. The evaluation included a review of documents, academic journal articles, financial and program data and, interviews with PHAC’s staff and stakeholders.
